description: A signed, chained, Merkle-verifiable audit trail. Every action gets one SHA3-256 hash, one Ed25519 signature, one chain link, one Merkle proof — and the whole thing verifies in any language with crypto primitives. No trusted audit service required.

records.summary: An agent was spawned. Every artifact below (the canonical bytes, the SHA3-256 hash, the Ed25519 signature, the Merkle path) is real and verifiable.
records.aftermath: The signed event is appended to the per-agent provenance log (jsonl on disk) AND the chain's Merkle root is updated. Hot tier: in-memory + WAL. Warm tier: rotated logs after N events. Cold tier: archived to S3 with the root hash retained.

beats.copy: An agent was spawned — event 1 of 5 in this trace. Every audit trail starts somewhere. This is the genesis event — no predecessor, just a public key and a starting hash.

Event content: agent_id "agent-orchestrator", trace_id "trace-2026-05-12-7f3a...", event_type "agent.spawn". Payload hashed to 0eb9efc80f67… — the payload itself stays out of the chain, only its hash.
Encode to canonical bytes: domain tag "PROV_EVENT_V1", schema version 1, length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. 174 bytes. Deterministic — re-encoding gives the same bytes byte-for-byte.

SHA3-256 hash of the canonical bytes: 1a0d142f385017…. Ed25519 signature over those same bytes: 09b28a9a3dde5f… (64 bytes). Attach the agent's public key: 735dc63d984a… (32 bytes). The signed event is now self-verifying.
Genesis event. prev_event_hash is null — there is no predecessor. The trace starts here.

Merkle proof for this event: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes total). The verifier walks the path from this leaf to the chain root (52376232fa7f…) using only those siblings. The proof grows logarithmically with the chain length.

Anyone with the agent's public key (735dc63d984a…) and the chain root (52376232fa7f…) can verify: signature verifies ✓, chain link links cleanly ✓, Merkle proof verifies against the chain root ✓. Three independent checks, all local, all crypto.

beats.copy: The agent read a file — event 2 of 5 in this trace. Every tool the agent runs becomes a signed event. The signature proves which agent did it; the chain link proves when, relative to everything else in this trace.

Chain link: prev_event_hash = 1a0d142f385017…, which is the leaf hash of event 1. The chain advances. Any reordering or insertion would break this link.

Merkle proof for this event: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes total). The verifier walks the path from this leaf to the chain root (52376232fa7f…) using only those siblings. The proof grows logarithmically with the chain length.

Anyone with the agent's public key (d6761a239028…) and the chain root (52376232fa7f…) can verify: signature verifies ✓, chain link links cleanly ✓, Merkle proof verifies against the chain root ✓. Three independent checks, all local, all crypto.

beats.copy: A tool call failed — event 4 of 5 in this trace. Failures get the same audit treatment as successes. The chain doesn't have a 'happy path' — every event is logged regardless of outcome.
Event content: agent_id "agent-tools", trace_id "trace-2026-05-12-7f3a...", event_type "tool.failed". Payload hashed to 0bdd02bfc5ed… — the payload itself stays out of the chain, only its hash.

records.plain: the agent despawned — then someone tampered with the record
records.summary: Signed honestly, then tampered with after the fact. The signature exposes the lie — anyone can verify this independently, with no audit service in the loop.
records.aftermath: The orchestrator's auditor flags the event on the next verify pass. The trace is preserved with the tamper recorded — the chain doesn't censor failed verifications, it just makes them undeniable. Downstream systems can decide what to do; the cryptographic fact is unambiguous.

beats.copy: The agent despawned — then someone tampered with the record — event 5 of 5 in this trace. This event was signed honestly. Then we mutated the metadata after the fact (someone trying to inflate the activity log). Signature verification catches it: the signature was over the original bytes, and the bytes don't match anymore.

Event content: agent_id "agent-orchestrator", trace_id "trace-2026-05-12-7f3a...", event_type "agent.despawn". Payload hashed to 38554786e330… — the payload itself stays out of the chain, only its hash.
Encode to canonical bytes: domain tag "PROV_EVENT_V1", schema version 1, length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. 209 bytes. Deterministic — re-encoding gives the same bytes byte-for-byte.

SHA3-256 hash of the canonical bytes: 0f6ee3cb2891ba…. Ed25519 signature over those same bytes: c1191cb48fcb16… (64 bytes). Attach the agent's public key: 735dc63d984a… (32 bytes). The signed event is now self-verifying.
Chain link: prev_event_hash = 481f95bbdc7bde…, which is the leaf hash of event 4. The chain advances. Any reordering or insertion would break this link.

Merkle proof for this event: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes total). The verifier walks the path from this leaf to the chain root (52376232fa7f…) using only those siblings. The proof grows logarithmically with the chain length.

This event was signed honestly. Then someone changed "tools_run" from "3" to "30". The signature was over the original bytes — the new bytes are not what got signed. Result: signature FAILS to verify ✗. The Merkle proof itself still verifies against the (tampered) leaf hash — but anyone re-checking the signature catches the lie. No central authority needed. No audit DB you have to trust.
